questions.question: Where does a String object created with `new String("hello")` reside?
options: String pool only
Stack only
Heap (new object), plus "hello" in the string pool
Metaspace
questions.explanation: Using `new String()` always creates a new object on the heap. The literal "hello" also exists in the string pool. The two are separate objects, which is why == comparison between them returns false.

questions.question: Which `var` declaration is INVALID?
options: var list = new ArrayList<String>();
var count = 0;
var x;
var name = "Alice";
questions.explanation: `var x;` is invalid because var requires an initializer on the right-hand side so the compiler can infer the type. Without initialization, type inference is impossible.

questions.question: What does `"hello".concat(" world")` do to the original String?
options: Modifies it in place to "hello world"
Nothing — it returns a new String; the original is unchanged
Throws UnsupportedOperationException
Appends to the string pool entry
questions.explanation: Strings are immutable in Java. All String methods that appear to modify content actually return a new String object. The original remains unchanged.

questions.question: How does Java pass object references to methods?
options: Pass by reference — the method can reassign the caller's variable
Pass by value of the reference — the method gets a copy of the pointer
Pass by copy — the entire object is cloned
It depends on whether the object is mutable or immutable
questions.explanation: Java is always pass-by-value. For objects, the value being passed is a copy of the reference (pointer). The method can modify the object through the reference, but cannot make the caller's variable point to a different object.

questions.question: Which is the recommended way to handle a method that might return no value?
options: Return null and check with if (result != null)
Return Optional<T> and use orElse() or ifPresent()
Throw an exception when no value is found
Return a default value of the type
questions.explanation: Optional<T> (Java 8+) explicitly signals that a value may be absent, making the code self-documenting and reducing NullPointerException risks. It provides methods like orElse(), map(), and ifPresent() for safe handling.
